retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid tranquilization of the agitated patient Alcohol withdrawal delirium and syndrome Insomnia Panic disorder Delirium Chemotherapy-associated anticipatory nausea and vomiting (adjunct or breakthrough) Psychogenic catatonia Vertigo [3] [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine medication approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for short-term relief of anxiety symptoms associated with anxiety disorders, anxiety-related insomnia, anesthesia premedication in adults to relieve anxiety or induce sedation or amnesia, and treatment of status epilepticus. Lorazepam is favored in inpatient settings for its rapid onset of action (1-3 minutes intravenously). Off-label uses for lorazepam include rapid tranquilization of agitated patients, alcohol withdrawal delirium and syndrome, insomnia, panic disorder, delirium, chemotherapy-associated anticipatory nausea and vomiting (adjunct or breakthrough), vertigo, and psychogenic catatonia. Lorazepam is used in various clinical practices and is often compared with other benzodiazepines. Clinicians from several specialties and at different levels of practice utilize it in acute, short-term, and long-term settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Clonazepam -- Monitoring -- Abrupt Discontinuation and Tolerance
retrieved_docs.content: Abrupt withdrawal of clonazepam should be avoided, particularly in those patients on long-term, high-dose therapy for a seizure disorder or other conditions, as it may result in status epilepticus and withdrawal symptoms. Withdrawal symptoms include anxiety, irritability, insomnia, tremors, headache, depression, sweating, confusion, hallucinations, and seizures. Long-term use also leads to developing tolerance, especially to its anticonvulsant properties, which can precipitate a seizure.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- History and Physical -- Barbiturates and Benzodiazepines
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms include agitation, poor memory, insomnia, anxiety, panic attacks, irritability, social phobia, and psychosis. Physical symptoms and signs include headache, gastrointestinal distress, muscle fatigue, weakness, tremor, and seizures. Autonomic instability with tachycardia, hypertension, and tachypnea occur in severe withdrawal. [16] [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Short-term (4 months) relief of anxiety symptoms associated with anxiety disorders Anxiety-related insomnia Anesthesia premedication in adults to relieve anxiety or to induce sedation or amnesia Treatment of status epilepticus [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Temazepam -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of discontinuation from temazepam is the same as that of any other benzodiazepine. Abrupt discontinuation of continuous treatment with benzodiazepines may lead to withdrawal or rebound syndrome. In contrast to a sole increase in benzodiazepine binding sites, an augmentation in the number of binding sites for both benzodiazepines and chloride-channel ligands suggests that the available number of GABA-A receptor complexes is heightened following benzodiazepine discontinuation. [52] Sleep lab studies have confirmed rebound insomnia following the sudden cessation of short-term, therapeutic benzodiazepine use as a hypnotic or anxiolytic. Temazepam induces rebound insomnia after abrupt discontinuation, which can contribute to relapse and continued drug use. [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Lorazepam is classified as a schedule IV drug, and patients may develop dependence and tolerance with prolonged use. Thus, utilizing the lowest effective dose of lorazepam for the shortest duration is advised. When discontinuing lorazepam, a tapering schedule of 0.5 mg every 3 days is recommended to mitigate withdrawal symptoms. [36] The dose of lorazepam should be monitored and adjusted according to the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol–Revised (CIWA-Ar) protocol. [37] Improvement in anxiety should be monitored using a validated scoring system such as the Hamilton Anxiety Scale. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Although not considered a withdrawal syndrome, abrupt discontinuation of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can lead to distressing symptoms termed SSRI drug discontinuation syndrome. Symptoms include gastrointestinal distress, dizziness, sleep disturbance, anxiety, irritability, and poor concentration. These symptoms develop between 1 to 7 days after discontinuation of the SSRI, after being on the medication for at least 30 days. This is not believed to be a remanifestation of the original disorder. Treatment included reinstituting the medication with a slow taper if discontinuation is to be achieved. [77] [78] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Fatal Familial Insomnia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Discontinuation of medications that may exacerbate confusion, memory, and insomnia is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: Temazepam -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals, including physicians, nurses, and pharmacists, must have a comprehensive understanding of the advantages and drawbacks associated with using temazepam as a therapeutic intervention for insomnia. Patients should only use temazepam, a benzodiazepine, for short-term sleep management, and this limitation should be communicated to them. In addition, patients should be aware that temazepam will be gradually tapered over a specific period to mitigate the risk of precipitating an acute withdrawal syndrome, which can lead to severe medical and neuropsychiatric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for lorazepam use based on patient presentation and clinical guidelines. Implement appropriate monitoring protocols to assess patient response and potential adverse reactions to lorazepam therapy. Select alternative treatment options or adjust lorazepam dosage based on individual patient characteristics and treatment goals. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare teams, including pharmacists and therapists, to ensure comprehensive patient care and treatment planning. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Lorazepam and other benzodiazepines are not recommended as first-line agents for anxiety and other psychiatric disorder symptoms during the first and third trimesters of pregnancy. Lorazepam and other benzodiazepines carry an elevated risk of abuse, misuse, and dependence. Consequently, they are contraindicated in patients who are actively using illicit substances and drugs. With the exception of patients undergoing detoxification for AUD, lorazepam and other benzodiazepines should not be used in individuals with a history of alcohol dependence or abuse who are not in remission. Combined use of alcohol and lorazepam in overdose poses an increased risk of fatality, including death. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Temazepam -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Quality of life is multifaceted, consisting of various physical, psychological, and social dimensions. Research has demonstrated that both lower sleep quantity and poor sleep quality have damaging effects on these dimensions. [76] [77] As noted above, insomnia frequently coexists with certain primary sleep disorders such as restless legs syndrome, periodic limb movement disorders, and sleep-related breathing disorders, which require referral to sleep medicine specialists. [78] Medical disorders leading to insomnia, such as hyperthyroidism, should be ruled out. [79] The decision to initiate temazepam should be carefully evaluated, considering medical and neuropsychiatric issues, as well as the potential to improve overall quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Florida Controlled Substance Prescribing -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Anxiety
retrieved_docs.content: Benzodiazepines are schedule IV  medications and include drugs such as alprazolam, clonazepam, diazepam, estazolam, and triazolam. They are used to treat anxiety, stress reactions, muscle spasms, and sleep disorders. They should be used short-term with caution as they have a high risk of addiction, dependence, and tolerance. The combination of benzodiazepines and opioids should be avoided due to the potential for addiction and respiratory depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Administration -- Adult Dosages
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder: The initial starting dosage of lorazepam is 2 to 3 mg orally, which may be repeated 2 to 3 times daily. The recommended daily dosage should not exceed 10 mg. [11] Insomnia due to anxiety or stress: The recommended dosage for lorazepam is 0.5 to 2 mg orally at bedtime for patients aged 65 or younger and 0.5 to 1 mg for patients aged 65 or older.

retrieved_docs.title: Temazepam -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
retrieved_docs.content: Developing a tolerance to temazepam is possible, especially if it is used for a moderate-to-long period. Symptoms and signs of temazepam withdrawal may include agitation, irritability, dysphoria, depersonalization, twitching, muscle cramps and discomfort, weakness, ataxia, headache, hypersensitivity to lights and other stimuli, nausea, sweating, and vomiting. Acute delirium, characterized by impaired attention and cognition, may be an ominous sign of temazepam withdrawal. Disruptions in sleep and mood have been identified as signs of withdrawal tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics, a therapeutic group of medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. As members of the same drug class, these agents share the primary indication of treating anxiety disorders, although they differ in chemical structure, pharmacokinetics and side‑effect profiles, which influences the choice of a specific anxiolytic for individual patient care. Understanding that each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, monitor for class‑related adverse effects, and provide comprehensive management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2217
retrieved_docs.content: Psychoactive substances are the causative agents of psychoactive substance withdrawal, meaning that when the use of a psychoactive substance is reduced or stopped, individuals may experience a withdrawal syndrome characterized by a range of physical and psychological symptoms. Recognizing that psychoactive substances precipitate withdrawal is essential for clinicians to anticipate, prevent, and manage these symptoms, thereby improving patient safety and supporting successful cessation or tapering strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to reduce anxiety symptoms, and each of these compounds is recognized as belonging to that class. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific clinical applications, they share the common purpose of alleviating anxiety, and selection among them in patient care depends on factors such as efficacy, side‑effect profile, and individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 390
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are both contraindicated for use in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that these antidepressant formulations should be avoided in this clinical context. In contrast, tofisopam may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential role for this anxiolytic agent in managing the symptoms associated with substance discontinuation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308, alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. As members of the anxiolytic drug class, each of these agents is used to alleviate anxiety symptoms and related disorders. Their shared classification reflects common therapeutic goals and, in many cases, similar mechanisms of action that target central nervous system pathways to produce calming effects. Recognizing that these diverse compounds belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate anxiety‑reducing treatments and understanding potential cross‑reactivity, side‑effect profiles, and drug‑interaction considerations within this medication class.

Their shared designation as anxiolytics reflects a common therapeutic purpose in managing anxiety disorders, despite differences in their specific chemical structures or pharmacologic sub‑classes.

Recognizing PD 134308 as part of this pharmacologic group highlights its likely mechanism of action and clinical use alongside the other listed anxiolytic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1319
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics comprise a diverse group of medications used to alleviate anxiety, and several specific agents are classified within this category. Bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, and estazolam are all identified as anxiolytic drugs. In addition, medazepam, midazolam, prazepam, and triazolam are likewise recognized as members of the anxiolytic class. Together, these agents represent a range of pharmacologic options—primarily benzodiazepine derivatives and a non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic (buspiron)—that clinicians may select to manage anxiety disorders and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756

Each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic drug class, indicating they are used to reduce anxiety symptoms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1563
retrieved_docs.content: Alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, chlormezanon, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, meprobamát and prazepam are all classified as anxiolytics. Each of these agents belongs to the broader class of drugs used to alleviate anxiety and related symptoms. Many of them, such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam and prazepam, are benzodiazepine derivatives, while others like buspiron and chlormezanon have distinct chemical structures but share the same therapeutic purpose. As members of the anxiolytic category, they are employed in clinical practice to manage anxiety disorders, provide sedation, and, in some cases, treat related conditions such as muscle tension or insomnia, highlighting their interconnected role in the pharmacologic management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class that includes several benzodiazepine agents—bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam—as well as the non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic buspiron. All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Buspiron, in contrast, exerts its anxiolytic action primarily through partial agonism at serotonin 5‑HT1A receptors, offering anxiety relief with a lower risk of sedation and dependence. Recognizing that these diverse agents all belong to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the patient’s specific needs, risk of side effects, and potential for dependence.

retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, alpidem, bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam and prazepam are all classified as anxiolytic agents. As members of the anxiolytic drug class, each of these compounds is used to alleviate anxiety symptoms and related disorders. Recognizing that these diverse agents share the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate anxiolytic therapy and understand their common mechanism of reducing central nervous system excitability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2289
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder caused by drug is classified as اضطراب القلق بسبب حالة طبية عامة, meaning it is a type of anxiety disorder that arises secondary to a general medical condition, specifically the pharmacologic effects of a medication. This relationship highlights that drug‑induced anxiety is considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders linked to underlying medical illnesses, emphasizing the importance of recognizing medication side effects as a distinct etiological factor in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) and nonspecific chest pain are clinically associated, indicating that patients experiencing anxiety may also present with nonspecific chest pain and vice versa. Pharmacotherapy is recognized as an appropriate and permitted treatment option for both anxiety and nonspecific chest pain, highlighting the relevance of medication management in addressing these interrelated conditions.
